sanskrit_devanagari: यो मां पश्यति सर्वत्र सर्वं च मयि पश्यति ।तस्याहं न प्रणश्यामि स च मे न प्रणश्यति ॥ ३० ॥
sanskrit_iast: yo māṁ paśyati sarvatrasarvaṁ ca mayi paśyatitasyāhaṁ na praṇaśyāmisa ca me na praṇaśyati

translations.verbatim_quote: For one who sees Me everywhere and sees everything in Me, I am never lost, nor is he ever lost to Me.
For one who sees Me everywhere and sees everything in Me, I am never lost, nor is he ever lost to Me.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 1749 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: A person in Kṛṣṇa consciousness certainly sees Lord Kṛṣṇa everywhere, and he sees everything in Kṛṣṇa. Such a person may appear to see all separate manifestations of the material nature, but in each and every instance he is conscious of Kṛṣṇa, knowing that everything is a manifestation of Kṛṣṇa’s energy.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 6.30 Yah, one who; pasyati, sees; mam, Me, Vasudeva, who am the Self of all; sarvatra, in all things; ca, and; sees sarvam, all things, all created things, beginning from Brahma; mayi, in Me who am the Self of all;-aham, I who am God; na pranasyami, do not go out; tasya,of his vision-of on

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 6.30 (ii) He who, having reached the highest stage of maturity, views similarity of nature with Me, i.e., sees similarity of all selves to Myself when They are freed from good and evil and when they remain in Their own essence, as declared in the Sruti, ‘Stainless he attains supreme degree

positions.rendering: For one who sees Me everywhere and sees everything in Me, I am never lost, nor is he ever lost to Me
one who sees Vasudeva (= Brahman) in all beings — to that one the Lord does not become invisible, nor does that one become invisible to the Lord
supreme seeing names the relationship of mutual non-abandonment between the bhakta and the Lord

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Shankara reads mām non-dually (Vasudeva = Brahman); Ramanuja and Prabhupada read mām theistically (Krishna as the personal Lord whose mutual non-abandonment with the devotee is named). The verse's full force is theistic on either reading; Shankara's reading subordinates the theism to non-dual Brahman, while Vaishnava traditions preserve the personal mutuality. The mutual non-abandonment is preserved across traditions.

literal_meaning: Whoever sees Me everywhere and sees everything in Me — to him I am not lost, and he is not lost to Me.
traditional_meaning_consensus: 6.30 marks the chapter's first explicitly theistic crystallisation. The previous verse named the ātman seen in all beings; here, mām (Me — Krishna) replaces ātman as the object of universal seeing. Yaḥ mām paśyati sarvatra (whoever sees Me everywhere), sarvam ca mayi paśyati (and sees everything in Me) — to that one, tasya aham na praṇaśyāmi (I am not lost), saḥ ca me na praṇaśyati (he is not lost to Me). Shankara: the bhakti-reading of equal vision — one who sees Vasudeva (= Brahman) everywhere is one whom the Lord does not abandon, and who does not abandon the Lord. Ramanuja: the supreme seeing names the relationship of mutual non-abandonment between the bhakta and the Lord. Per chapter-6 thesis: the verse is theistic — STRETCHED. Engineering analog operates at substrate-recognition scope: the senior sees the operating substrate (the actual system, the actual code) across every surface interaction. The verse's full devotional reach exceeds operational analog.
